title: Online Fraud: First Steps to Protect Yourself and Your Case
author: Adv. Sneha Kapoor
content: When money is lost to an online scam, people often rush to share screenshots and messages in multiple places. The best first step is to preserve evidence carefully: keep transaction IDs, bank SMS alerts, call logs, chat history, email headers, and any URLs involved.

Report quickly through the national cybercrime portal and your bank’s fraud channel. Timing matters for freezing or reversing transactions, especially in UPI and card disputes. Keep a written timeline of events—date, time, platform, and what you clicked or shared.
Avoid editing screenshots or forwarding sensitive OTP messages. If you need to share evidence, share a copy and keep the original intact. For certain matters, metadata can be important later.

If the fraud involved impersonation, threats, or extortion, consider a legal notice or additional complaint route after initial reporting. The right approach depends on the platform and the type of loss.
A focused, documented approach reduces confusion and helps authorities act faster. The goal is not volume of messages—it is clarity and traceability.
